nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon, channel, and protected seaward reef slopes; generally found at depths of about 1–25 m where there is rock and coral rubble for grazing and shelter

diet.notes: Has been documented grazing on algae, small crustaceans, and worms in the wild. In captivity, generally advised to offer a varied diet including spirulina, marine algae (nori sheets), high-quality angelfish preparations enriched with sponge material, and meaty foods such as mysis or frozen shrimp. Multiple small feedings per day are commonly recommended, and ample live rock for supplemental grazing tends to support long-term health.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 2639x1979; tack-sharp full-body side profile in situ, vivid yellow front half, deep blue rear, yellow tail and blue head crown clearly shown.
Diego Delso (Anilao, Philippines); 2646x1764; crisp full-body against clean blue water, vibrant bicolor pattern and diagnostic head crown, different individual/setting for variety.
Bernard DUPONT; 3312x1987; sharp in-situ reef shot showing whole fish grazing near soft coral, natural-habitat context.

summary: A striking Indo-Pacific dwarf angelfish with vivid yellow on the front half and deep blue on the rear; generally considered semi-aggressive, with grazing habits that can put soft and stony corals at risk in reef tanks.
careNotes: The Bicolor Angelfish is one of the larger Centropyge dwarf angels and has a reputation for being moderately challenging to acclimate — newly imported specimens have been known to refuse food for days or weeks, so it is commonly advised to source well-established, actively-feeding individuals from reputable suppliers. An aquarium with mature live rock for grazing tends to make the difference between thriving and pining away. It is best introduced last to reduce territorial pressure. Generally advised to keep only one per tank; mated pairs can occasionally be kept together but harem attempts typically fail in home-sized aquariums. The species is considered reef safe with caution: it has been reported to nip at stony and soft coral polyps, clam mantles, and zoanthids, and is generally best avoided in tanks dedicated to prized reef specimens. Sex reversal (protogyny — a natural process in which a fish changes from female to male) has been documented in the species and is reportedly completed in about 18–20 days when a dominant male is removed from a group.
breedingNotes: Has very rarely been bred in home aquariums. Like most Centropyge, the species is a protogynous hermaphrodite — the largest fish in a harem becomes male. No reliable external sexual dimorphism in coloration has been reported. Reproduces in the wild as a pelagic spawner, releasing eggs and sperm into the water column. Larval rearing in captivity has been attempted but generally considered impractical at the hobbyist level.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (15 cm TL max vs LiveAquaria ~10 cm trade size; 10–15 cm range adopted), temperature (25–29.3°C wild range vs LiveAquaria 72–78°F/22–26°C; conservative middle 23–28°C used), native range (Indo-Pacific: East Africa to Samoan/Phoenix Islands, north to southern Japan, south to New Caledonia), taxonomy (Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes), lifespan max 13 years, protogynous hermaphrodite with 18–20 day sex reversal, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: AquariumSource / hobby cross-check
additional.notes: Hobby cross-check for tank-size spread: hobby sources range 55–75 gal (208–284 L). Per playbook tank-size rule, the cautious-larger 300 L (~80 gal) value was chosen because this is the largest Centropyge and benefits from open swimming space alongside abundant grazing rockwork.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite; pelagic spawner. Captive reproduction is very rarely achieved at the hobbyist level.
